commit 0acc5b5ad9e5adb123dd7bb80ba997ed6460605b
Author: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>

mm: vmscan: fix dirty folios throttling on cgroup v1 for MGLRU

This commit enables dirty folio throttling on cgroup v1 for MGLRU by
checking if evicted file folios are predominantly dirty and unqueued,
waking the flusher, and throttling reclaim.

> diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c
> index 544ff1374ae6..fe89fff85c18 100644
> --- a/mm/vmscan.c
> +++ b/mm/vmscan.c
> @@ -5023,9 +5023,24 @@ static bool try_to_shrink_lruvec(struct lruvec *lruvec, struct scan_control *sc)
>  	 * If too many file cache in the coldest generation can't be evicted
>  	 * due to being dirty, wake up the flusher.
>  	 */
> -	if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken)
> +	if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken) {
> +		struct pglist_data *pgdat = lruvec_pgdat(lruvec);
>  
>  		wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN);
>  
> +		/*
> +		 * For cgroupv1 dirty throttling is achieved by waking up
> +		 * the kernel flusher here and later waiting on folios
> +		 * which are in writeback to finish (see shrink_folio_list()).
> +		 *
> +		 * Flusher may not be able to issue writeback quickly
> +		 * enough for cgroupv1 writeback throttling to work
> +		 * on a large system.
> +		 */
> +		if (!writeback_throttling_sane(sc))
> +			reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);
> +	}
> +
>  	/* whether this lruvec should be rotated */
>  	return nr_to_scan < 0;
>  }

Will evaluating sc->nr.unqueued_dirty and sc->nr.file_taken here cause
over-throttling stalls or disable throttling entirely?

The sc->nr fields are global accumulators that collect statistics across
the entire shrink_node() pass and are not zeroed between memcg iterations.
Because try_to_shrink_lruvec() is invoked repeatedly for each memory cgroup,
could these accumulators carry over state from previous memcgs?

If an early memcg yields only unqueued dirty file folios,
sc->nr.unqueued_dirty and sc->nr.file_taken become equal. For all subsequent
memcgs that yield zero file folios, these global accumulators will remain
unchanged and equal. Could this cause the reclaimer to mistakenly sleep
for every such memcg, creating multi-second latency spikes in a deep
hierarchy?

Conversely, if any memcg yields even a single clean file folio,
sc->nr.file_taken will permanently exceed sc->nr.unqueued_dirty. Does this
permanently disable the throttle condition for the remainder of the entire
node scan, even if millions of unqueued dirty folios are encountered later?

Classic LRU avoids this by evaluating local batch statistics rather than
global accumulators.

commit 0acc5b5ad9e5adb123dd7bb80ba997ed6460605b
Author: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>
Date:   Fri Mar 27 10:21:08 2026 +0000

    mm: vmscan: fix dirty folios throttling on cgroup v1 for MGLRU
    
    The balance_dirty_pages() won't do the dirty folios throttling on cgroupv1.
    See commit 9badce000e2c ("cgroup, writeback: don't enable cgroup writeback
    on traditional hierarchies").
    
    Moreover, after commit 6b0dfabb3555 ("fs: Remove aops->writepage"), we no
    longer attempt to write back filesystem folios through reclaim.
    
    On large memory systems, the flusher may not be able to write back quickly
    enough. Consequently, MGLRU will encounter many folios that are already
    under writeback. Since we cannot reclaim these dirty folios, the system
    may run out of memory and trigger the OOM killer.
    
    Hence, for cgroup v1, let's throttle reclaim after waking up the flusher,
    which is similar to commit 81a70c21d917 ("mm/cgroup/reclaim: fix dirty
    pages throttling on cgroup v1"), to avoid unnecessary OOM.
    
    The following test program can easily reproduce the OOM issue. With this patch
    applied, the test passes successfully.
    
    $mkdir /sys/fs/cgroup/memory/test
    $echo 256M > /sys/fs/cgroup/memory/test/memory.limit_in_bytes
    $echo $$ > /sys/fs/cgroup/memory/test/cgroup.procs
    $dd if=/dev/zero of=/mnt/data.bin bs=1M count=800
    
    Fixes: ac35a4902374 ("mm: multi-gen LRU: minimal implementation")
    Reviewed-by: Barry Song <baohua@kernel.org>
    Reviewed-by: Kairui Song <kasong@tencent.com>
    Signed-off-by: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>

diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c
index 544ff1374ae6..fe89fff85c18 100644
--- a/mm/vmscan.c
+++ b/mm/vmscan.c
@@ -5023,9 +5023,24 @@ static bool try_to_shrink_lruvec(struct lruvec *lruvec, struct scan_control *sc)
 	 * If too many file cache in the coldest generation can't be evicted
 	 * due to being dirty, wake up the flusher.
 	 */
-	if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken)
+	if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken) {
+		struct pglist_data *pgdat = lruvec_pgdat(lruvec);
+
 		wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN);
 
+		/*
+		 * For cgroupv1 dirty throttling is achieved by waking up
+		 * the kernel flusher here and later waiting on folios
+		 * which are in writeback to finish (see shrink_folio_list()).
+		 *
+		 * Flusher may not be able to issue writeback quickly
+		 * enough for cgroupv1 writeback throttling to work
+		 * on a large system.
+		 */
+		if (!writeback_throttling_sane(sc))
+			reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);
+	}
+
 	/* whether this lruvec should be rotated */
 	return nr_to_scan < 0;
 }

    "thought": "**MGLRU and Dirty Folio Throttling in cgroup v1: A Deep Dive**

Okay, so the commit message flags an issue: MGLRU is struggling with dirty folio throttling in cgroup v1. The problem stems from `balance_dirty_pages()` not handling dirty folios throttling in cgroup v1 and the removal of `aops->writepage`, which used to handle writing back filesystem folios via reclaim. On large memory systems, the flusher can't keep up, leading to a backlog of folios already in writeback. This is a problem because we can't reclaim these dirty folios, which can cause an OOM situation.

The proposed solution attempts to add writeback throttling for cgroup v1 within `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`. The logic is that if `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` equals `sc->nr.file_taken`, we wake up the flusher threads. Now, the key is the conditional: if `!writeback_throttling_sane(sc)` (which is true for cgroup v1), then `reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK)` is called.

The use of `reclaim_throttle()` is a potential problem, because it's a blocking call that essentially stalls the reclaimer. That seems like it could create problems. Looking at the code:

```c
	if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken) {
		struct pglist_data *pgdat = lruvec_pgdat(lruvec);

		wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN);

		if (!writeback_throttling_sane(sc))
			reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);
	}
```

Is `writeback_throttling_sane()` a standard check for cgroup v1?

Let's look at the patch again to check.

```c

		/*
		 * For cgroupv1 dirty throttling is achieved by waking up
		 * the kernel flusher here and later waiting on folios

		 *
		 * Flusher may not be able to issue writeback quickly

		 * on a large system.

		if (!writeback_throttling_sane(sc))
			reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);
	}
```

```

    "thought": "**MGLRU Dirty Throttling Logic Flaw**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought was to examine the `shrink_one()` and `shrink_many()` functions, and how they interact with `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`.  I needed to understand where the key counters, `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken`, are initialized, particularly in the context of the proposed MGLRU patch. I recalled how the classic LRU implementation handles this in `shrink_inactive_list()` where stats are accumulated into `sc->nr`.

In classic LRU, I found that `shrink_inactive_list()` uses a local `struct reclaim_stat stat`. Crucially, it accumulates the relevant statistics into `sc->nr` within `shrink_inactive_list()` itself. This included counters for dirty pages, unqueued dirty pages, etc., and also the number of taken pages (file pages in particular). The dirty throttling logic in classic LRU, uses local variables like `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty == nr_taken` which is done within the `shrink_inactive_list()`.

The MGLRU patch, however, uses the accumulated stats in `sc->nr`. The key difference is the check `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken`. I need to confirm if these stats are reset before each call to `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`. A quick look at the initialization of `sc` in `do_try_to_free_pages()`, `kswapd()`, or `balance_pgdat()` shows that `sc` is zero-initialized. Therefore, `sc->nr` accumulates stats across the entire reclaim session, across *all* LRU vectors, nodes, and memcgs!

This is where the problem arises.  Consider the iteration process. In one call to `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`, `file_taken` might be 100, and `unqueued_dirty` is 0. If in the next iteration, `file_taken` becomes 200, and `unqueued_dirty` becomes 100, then the check `100 == 200` is false. If `file_taken` is initially 100 and `unqueued_dirty` 100, then it's true, we are throttled. If in the second iteration, `file_taken` becomes 110 (from clean pages) and `unqueued_dirty` is still 100, the condition `100 == 110` is false. This is fundamentally different from the per-batch, local variable check in classic LRU, which looks at the state of the *current* scan.

Now, with MGLRU's `evict_folios()`, it scans both anonymous and file folios within `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`. Since `sc->nr.file_taken` and `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` are global to the entire reclaim session, *any* clean file pages taken early in the reclaim process will permanently invalidate the throttling condition. This means if, at any point, `file_taken` is greater than `unqueued_dirty`, the throttle condition `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` will never be met for the rest of that `sc`'s lifetime. This renders the throttling logic almost entirely ineffective if any clean file pages were scanned. This is a critical flaw.

    "thought": "**MGLRU Throttling Bug Analysis**

Okay, so I'm diving into this MGLRU patch, specifically the throttling logic, and here's what I'm seeing: The `struct scan_control sc` is initialized once per top-level reclaim caller, like `try_to_free_pages`, and it looks like it is intended to be the central state for the reclaim session. The problem, I think, lies in how the counters `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken` are used.

Classic LRU accumulates these counters as well within the `shrink_inactive_list()` function. However, the classic LRU *doesn't* actually use them for throttling. Instead, classic LRU uses local variables within `shrink_inactive_list()` to determine if throttling is needed, checking if `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty == nr_taken` where `stat` is local to the function. It's essentially checking if all the pages it *just* took from the file LRU were dirty and not undergoing writeback.

Now, MGLRU, it seems, is making a critical mistake. It uses the *global* cumulative `sc` counters in this condition: `if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken)`. If during the *entire* reclaim session, the system has encountered some clean file folios earlier, then `sc->nr.file_taken` will inevitably be greater than `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty`. Later, if it only encounters dirty file folios, `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` will increase, but it will *never* equal `sc->nr.file_taken` because of the earlier clean ones. Therefore, throttling will NEVER happen if any clean file folios were scanned in the *entire* reclaim session.

Conversely, if the session *started* with `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` (because it scanned only dirty pages), it will throttle. But it might throttle on every subsequent iteration where it finds dirty pages, or it might stop throttling forever as soon as it finds one clean page.

I had to double-check that MGLRU wasn't using local variables anywhere, but it's clear: The critical check happens *after* the `evict_folios()` loop. It's comparing the accumulated global values in `sc`.

Wait, let me make sure I'm following what those `sc` counters actually represent. Where are they reset? They are *never* reset during the lifetime of `sc`! So, `sc->nr.file_taken` is the total number of file folios isolated by `scan_folios()`. And `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` is the total number of unqueued dirty folios isolated by `scan_folios()` PLUS those returned by `shrink_folio_list()`. Wait...

In `scan_folios()`: it ONLY increments `sc->nr.file_taken` for dirty file folios? Yes!

This is a major bug. The condition is comparing global accumulators rather than the local batch stats! The throttling logic is fundamentally broken, and it’s going to lead to incorrect behavior during page reclaim. It will either not throttle when it should or it will throttle every time it encounters some dirty pages even if many clean pages have been previously scanned.

Okay, let's break this down. My initial reaction to `sort_folio()` was, "Hmm, this looks interesting." Now that I've gotten deeper into the code, I see a specific pattern emerging. It looks like `sort_folio()` has a nuanced role. The key seems to be the conditional increment of `sc->nr.file_taken`. It's only incremented *directly* within `sort_folio()` when a file folio is dirty. And if it's dirty, it's not isolated.

The interesting bit is that if a file folio *isn't* dirty (and it's not currently undergoing writeback), `sort_folio()` returns `false`. This means `scan_folios()` then steps in and calls `isolate_folio()`. And `isolate_folio()` isolates the folio. Then, within `scan_folios()`, after the isolation process, we find `sc->nr.file_taken` being incremented, but *this* time, it's via the `isolated` variable.

So, in summary, there are two distinct ways a file folio can influence `sc->nr.file_taken`:

1.  **Dirty/Writeback Folios:** These are handled *directly* in `sort_folio()`. The count is incremented *before* isolation, and they are not isolated.
2.  **Clean Folios:** These are *isolated* by `scan_folios()`, and then `sc->nr.file_taken` is incremented in `scan_folios()`.

Okay, that's it. It's a nice neat split of responsibilities and a clever accounting scheme. Time to look more closely at `scan_folios()` and see how the `isolated` variable is computed.

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**MGLRU Writeback Throttling: A Critical Flaw in Global Accumulation**

Okay, so here's the deal: I've been digging into the writeback throttling mechanism in MGLRU, and it's pretty clear there's a serious problem. The core issue lies in how MGLRU accumulates data for its throttling decision, and it's a fundamental misunderstanding of how this is supposed to work.

Let's break it down: `scan_folios()`, `sort_folio()`, and `shrink_folio_list()` all feed into these accumulators `sc->nr.file_taken` and `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty`.  `sc->nr.file_taken` basically tracks *every* file folio encountered (isolated, dirty, etc.) during a reclaim cycle.  `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` tracks the unqueued dirty file folios. The critical check is `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` to trigger throttling.

In classic LRU, throttling is *local* to a batch of pages.  If, in a batch of 32 folios, *all* are dirty, we throttle. This uses batch statistics. That makes perfect sense.

But MGLRU uses *global* accumulators, `sc->nr` within `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`. This `sc` lives for the entire reclaim invocation. This is where things go off the rails. Even if the system sees a few clean file folios at any point during `do_try_to_free_pages()`, `sc->nr.file_taken` immediately becomes larger than `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty`.  So, if you scan 100 clean folios and then 1,000,000 dirty folios, the comparison of the two,  `1000000 == 1000100` becomes *permanently* false for the rest of that reclaim invocation.  This makes the writeback throttling useless and that 1,000,000 dirty folios won't get throttled! This is a total regression.

Here's the kicker: MGLRU's `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` receives `struct scan_control *sc`, and it *never* clears these accumulators. The same `sc` struct is used for multiple calls to various reclaim functions throughout the entire cycle. The author misinterprets this global state as per-batch statistics.

The problem is particularly acute because dirty file folios in MGLRU are often handled directly in `sort_folio()` (skipping isolation and `shrink_folio_list()`). This means that their counts are added directly to the *global* `sc->nr.file_taken` and `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` accumulators.

So, in classic LRU, local batch stats are used, in MGLRU, global accumulators across all zones, memcgs, and generations are used. This makes throttling useless.

But there is another issue: The code repeatedly calls `reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK)` in a while loop. So if the system *only* sees dirty pages, then EVERY call to `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` will repeatedly call this throttle function, once per memcg, causing significant delays. Thus it is an "all or nothing" mechanism.

In summary, this is a serious flaw. The logic is fundamentally broken, the throttling mechanism is essentially disabled, and it introduces a design flaw with the global state accumulation.

    "thought": "**Analysis of the LRU/MGLRU Integration in Memory Management**

Okay, I've got it. Let's break this down. My initial observation was about the initialization of `sc->nr` within `shrink_node()`. Specifically, the call to `memset(&sc->nr, 0, sizeof(sc->nr));`. The critical point here is *where* that reset happens.

First, I confirmed it's called at the *beginning* of `shrink_node()`.  `shrink_node()` is the top-level function for a node's memory reclaim. `shrink_node()` then calls `shrink_node_memcgs()`, which iterates through each memcg on that node, and within that iteration calls `shrink_lruvec()` that eventually calls `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`. The initial `memset` indicates `sc->nr` *should* be fresh for each top level call of `shrink_node()`. The issue is how this is used within this structure.

In *classic LRU*, the relevant counters in `sc->nr` (like `writeback`, `immediate`, `dirty`, etc.) are *only* evaluated once per node at the end of `shrink_node()`. This is used to determine congestion or if the node is under heavy writeback.  For throttling writeback, it's evaluated in two places: at the LRU batch level in `shrink_inactive_list()` and again at the node level in `shrink_node()`.

However, the patch author is injecting throttling logic within `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`, which runs on a per-memcg basis *within* the node's reclaim path. They're doing this using the condition `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken`. The problem is that `sc->nr` is *accumulating* across memcgs.

Here's the problem: since `sc->nr` is reset at the beginning of `shrink_node()`, inside `shrink_node_memcgs()`, when we iterate over memcgs: For the first memcg, the counters are correct. But for the second memcg, the counters are the sum of the first *and* second memcgs!  For the third, the sum of the first, second, *and* third! This means that `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` is checking the condition using the *cumulative* stats of *all previously scanned memcgs* plus the current memcg.

This is a complete logic error!  If the first memcg scans 100 clean folios, the second memcg will *never* meet the condition (even if it's 100% dirty) because `file_taken` will include the 100 clean folios from the first memcg!  And if the first memcg is throttled, then even if the second memcg has 0 folios, it will see the numbers from the first memcg and trigger throttling again. This behavior is fundamentally flawed. The counters aren't being reset appropriately at each memcg level.

Now, wait! MGLRU takes a completely different path. When it's global reclaim (kswapd or global direct reclaim), and `root_reclaim()` is true, the code calls `lru_gen_shrink_node()` *instead* of `shrink_node_memcgs()`. Critically, `lru_gen_shrink_node()` performs its *own* memcg iteration internally! Thus, the accumulation doesn't happen, the numbers are correct, the counters work.

Let's trace `lru_gen_shrink_node()` which ultimately calls `shrink_many()`.

There you have it.

    "thought": "**Analysis of the MGLRU Writeback Throttling Implementation**

Okay, here's my breakdown of this MGLRU writeback throttling issue. As an expert, I'm thinking through this in a very specific way.

First, I realize that the `shrink_many()` function calls `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` repeatedly for different lruvecs. Now, critically, `shrink_many()` doesn't clear `sc->nr`, and neither does `shrink_one()` or `lru_gen_shrink_lruvec()`. This means, and this is the core of the problem, that within a single `shrink_node()` call, the `sc->nr` accumulator grows *across all memcgs* in the node! This breaks the original intention of the classic LRU behavior.

If MGLRU is reclaiming from the root memcg, it's calling `lru_gen_shrink_node()`, which cascades down through `shrink_many()` to iterate over memcgs. For each memcg, `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` is called. *That's* where the problematic logic exists.

Specifically, inside `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`, there's a check: `if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken)`. This condition is evaluating a *cumulative* count across the *entire node*, from the start of the `shrink_node()` process! This is a massive issue.

Think about what this means:
*   The throttling condition is applied on *global*, not local batch statistics, which is fundamentally wrong.
*   This global check happens *multiple times* (once per memcg!).
*   If the *first* memcg is all dirty, the code throttles (as expected, sort of). But, if the next memcg yields zero folios, the accumulated `sc->nr` stats are unchanged, *and* the code mistakenly throttles *again*.
*   Worse, if any memcg yields *even one clean file folio*, `file_taken` becomes larger than `unqueued_dirty`. After that, the throttling condition will *never* trigger again for the rest of the node scan, even if millions of dirty folios exist in subsequent memcgs! This is a disaster.

In essence, the throttle logic becomes permanently disabled after encountering a single clean file folio. This is completely broken.

The original intent was to replicate the behavior of the classic LRU where the throttling check would evaluate only local batch statistics, isolating dirty folio throttling to the *current batch* of scanned folios. Instead, `sc->nr` is global and accumulative, which is not what was intended and leads to this erratic behavior.

I will also check `writeback_throttling_sane(sc)` for cgroup v1 to confirm that it's consistent. This appears to be correct and aligns with classic LRU's handling. Also the condition `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` is safely guarded.

My other thought is this: in `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`, the code calls `evict_folios()`. Perhaps the intent was to check the local batch statistics within this function instead. However, the problem lies in that MGLRU has no single place to check the local batch stats because `shrink_folio_list()` isn't used for all dirty file folios and stats aren't easily collected. While `evict_folios()` could *potentially* throttle based on local stats, the problem is that `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` evaluates the global states repeatedly. This causes the architectural flaw.

I've checked and confirmed this: `shrink_node_memcgs()` calls multiple memcgs and the `sc->nr` value is **not** cleared between them, this ensures that the counters are globally scoped over the node.

I can definitively say that the throttling logic is fatally flawed. It will incorrectly throttle and incorrectly disable throttling, completely defeating the patch's purpose. I'll summarize it as an architectural mismatch that breaks the local batch throttle semantics.

      "type": "Logic Bug / Broken Throttling Mechanism",
      "description": "The writeback throttling condition incorrectly compares global `scan_control` accumulators instead of local batch statistics, rendering it ineffective in almost all scenarios.",
      "reasoning": "In classic LRU (`shrink_inactive_list()`), dirty folio throttling evaluates the condition `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty == nr_taken` using variables local to the current batch of isolated folios. It checks if the *current batch* is entirely composed of unqueued dirty folios and throttles if so. \n\nThis patch attempts to replicate this behavior in MGLRU's `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` but checks `if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken)`. The fields in `struct scan_control` (`sc->nr`) are global counters that accumulate statistics across the entire `shrink_node()` pass (across all loops and all memcgs). They are zeroed only once at the beginning of `shrink_node()`, not per memcg or per `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` call.\n\nBecause `shrink_many()` and `shrink_one()` repeatedly call `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` across multiple memcgs without clearing `sc->nr`:\n1. If any memcg yields even a single clean file folio, `sc->nr.file_taken` will permanently exceed `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` for the remainder of the node scan. Consequently, the throttling condition will permanently evaluate to false, failing to throttle even if millions of unqueued dirty folios are encountered in subsequent memcgs.\n2. Conversely, if the first scanned memcg yields only dirty folios, the condition is met and it throttles. If the next memcg yields 0 folios, the global `sc->nr` counters remain unchanged, causing it to incorrectly throttle *again* based on the previous memcg's stats.\n\nThis fundamentally misinterprets the purpose of `sc->nr` accumulators and results in unpredictable, predominantly ineffective writeback throttling."
    }
  ]

This is likely due to the changes introduced in commit `9badce000e2c`, which disables cgroup writeback on traditional hierarchies.

Essentially, the fix they're proposing involves throttling reclaim *after* waking up the flusher threads, which is similar to what was done in commit `81a70c21d917`. This should prevent unnecessary OOM situations.

The actual code change itself is in the condition block:

```c
if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken) {
    struct pglist_data *pgdat = lruvec_pgdat(lruvec);
    wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN);

    /*
     * For cgroupv1 dirty throttling is achieved by waking up
     * the kernel flusher here and later waiting on folios
     * which are in writeback to finish (see shrink_folio_list()).
     *
     * Flusher may not be able to issue writeback quickly
     * enough for cgroupv1 writeback throttling to work
     * on a large system.
     */
    if (!writeback_throttling_sane(sc))
        reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);
}
```

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Analyzing Writeback Throttling in VM Reclaim**

Okay, here's what's running through my head as I look at this code. In `shrink_inactive_list`, I see this comparison: `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty == nr_taken`. This is pretty straightforward within the context of a single list and iteration. Basically, if all the folios we *took* from the list this time around were unqueued dirty, we kick off flusher threads and maybe throttle writeback. Makes sense so far. It uses `stat`, a local variable specific to this list processing.

Now, my focus shifts to `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`, and I notice it's doing something similar but with `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken`. Immediately, alarm bells start ringing. Is it using *cumulative* counters? Are these `sc->nr` counters initialized per reclaim invocation or are they accumulating across multiple invocations? I need to know this!

If I understand this correctly, this `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` condition means that if *all* file folios processed *so far* during this entire `scan_control` run were unqueued dirty, we'll throttle and wake up flushers.

But here's the kicker: `sc->nr.file_taken` is incremented for *every* file folio, even the clean ones that are simply being evicted! Let's say we evicted 100 clean file folios, and then we come across 50 unqueued dirty file folios. `sc->nr.file_taken` will now be 150, but `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` will only be 50. So, `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` would be FALSE, and the throttling and flusher wakeup would *not* happen.

This feels wrong. `shrink_inactive_list` is local and fine, using `stat`. It's only throttled if all of the current folios are dirty. `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` appears to be using the global `sc` counters which are being incremented across all the folios encountered so far. That seems like a bug.
I'm going to have to dive into where `sc->nr` is initialized and see exactly how these counters are being used throughout the code. I need to trace the initialization and usage of `sc->nr` to be sure.


",

Okay, so I'm digging into this memory reclaim code, and I need to solidify my understanding of how `sc->nr` is being used and reset. Right off the bat, I see that `sc->nr` is zeroed out at the start of each `shrink_node()` iteration. That's a key observation: it gets reset per node-level scan, meaning it spans *all* memory cgroups (memcgs) within a single priority loop for a node. 

I'm following the flow... the code zeros out `sc->nr`, then initializes some counters based on `sc->nr`, sets up the scan control, and then calls `shrink_node_memcgs()`. Inside that function, it loops through each `memcg` in the node. Each `memcg` then calls `shrink_lruvec()`

`shrink_node_memcgs()` has the loop:
```c
	do {
		struct lruvec *lruvec = mem_cgroup_lruvec(memcg, pgdat);

		shrink_lruvec(lruvec, sc);

		if (!current_is_kswapd())
			sc->nr_reclaimed += current->reclaim_state->reclaimed;
		...
	} while ((memcg = mem_cgroup_iter(target_memcg, memcg, partial)));
```

So `sc->nr` is accumulating the counts across *all* memcgs scanned within the node at this priority level. But, hang on. I see MGLRU's got its own loop:

```c
	if ((lru_gen_enabled() || lru_gen_switching()) && root_reclaim(sc)) {
		memset(&sc->nr, 0, sizeof(sc->nr));
		lru_gen_shrink_node(pgdat, sc);
        ...
```

It re-zeros the `sc->nr`, and calls `lru_gen_shrink_node`. This function also does its own thing, including iterating through memcgs internally. This is a crucial distinction.

Now I'm focusing on what happens in `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`...
```c
static bool try_to_shrink_lruvec(struct lruvec *lruvec, struct scan_control *sc)
```
...because at the end of `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`, it checks `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken`! But here's the kicker: `sc->nr` isn't reset *per* `lruvec`. Instead, it *accumulates* across multiple `lruvec`s. 

If it accumulates, that means the equality check `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` could be true even if the individual `lruvec`s don't satisfy that condition on their own, *as long as* all prior `lruvec`s also had their `unqueued_dirty` equal to `file_taken`. I'm comparing this to the classic LRU behavior, where `shrink_inactive_list()` checks `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty == nr_taken`. But `stat` is local to a *single* `lruvec`'s batch. This is a per-batch check.

Here, MGLRU is using the *global* `sc->nr` to span across all batches, all `lruvec`s for the equality check at the end of each `lruvec`. I need to be absolutely certain of this. `sc->nr` is accumulating across batches, and MGLRU is checking it at the end of *shrinking a single* `lruvec`!

Time to carefully look around and see if `sc->nr` is reset anywhere else in the MGLRU code path, to make sure I haven't missed a crucial reset.

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought was about `lru_gen_shrink_node()` and whether it resets `sc->nr` per memcg. Turns out it doesn't. My focus shifted to `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` within MGLRU, specifically this line: `if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken)`. I immediately realized this function is called per `lruvec`, which translates to per memcg-node combination. And here's the kicker: `sc->nr.file_taken` represents the *cumulative* count across potentially multiple memcgs within a node priority loop!

So, if memcg A has 100 clean file folios, and memcg B has 50 dirty file folios, `sc->nr.file_taken` becomes 150, but `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` is 50. The crucial check `50 == 150` fails, and memcg B's throttling might not kick in, even though all its folios are dirty. This is the problem.

My next thought was about `sc->nr` reset: it's reset per *node priority loop* in `shrink_node()` before the memcg iteration, not per `lruvec`. So, `sc->nr` tracks node-level (or memcg-level if target memcg is set) stats across the whole priority iteration!

In classic LRU, dirty throttling in `shrink_inactive_list()` uses a condition where `nr_taken` is the number of folios taken in that specific batch. In MGLRU, `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` means the condition is ONLY true if *every single file folio taken on this node (or memcg tree) across all lruvecs processed so far in this priority loop* was unqueued dirty.

The condition will be false and we will skip throttling if even one clean file folio was taken previously. I needed to check if this was a bug. It turns out the condition `if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken)` was ALREADY THERE!

The patch just adds `if (!writeback_throttling_sane(sc)) reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);` inside the preexisting `if` block.

Is the patch itself the issue? It appears that if `writeback_throttling_sane(sc)` returns `false`, we call `reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);`. This matches the behavior in `shrink_inactive_list()`.

The commit message confirms that this is about cgroupv1 dirty throttling, to avoid unnecessary OOM by throttling reclaim after waking up the flusher.

I needed to make sure there are no locking issues:
`try_to_shrink_lruvec()` runs without `lruvec_lock_irq(lruvec)` held at that point. No spinlocks are held here. So `reclaim_throttle` is perfectly safe to call here because we are not holding any spinlock, nor are we in an RCU read side critical section.

Is there another potential issue? If `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` logic triggers, I needed to re-read `shrink_inactive_list()` logic.

Okay, let me break this down. My initial thought was to compare how writeback throttling is handled in the classic LRU and MGLRU. In classic LRU, `shrink_inactive_list` checks `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty == nr_taken` which is done on a *per-batch* basis.  If true, it throttles writeback with `reclaim_throttle`. This check happens repeatedly during `shrink_node()` iterations, which makes sense.

In MGLRU, `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` seems to have a similar check: `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` *after* the folio eviction loop.  But, wait, `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` is called *multiple times* for *different* `lruvec`s through `shrink_one()` which in turn is called from `shrink_node_memcgs()`. I'm starting to see a potential problem here. The crucial question is whether `sc->nr.file_taken` uses an accumulated count across *all* `lruvec`s in the current priority level. And the answer is yes: `sc->nr` is not reset *between* memcgs within `shrink_node_memcgs()`.

Here's the problem: consider the code snippet: `if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken)`. If, during a scan involving multiple memcgs, we encounter clean file folios in the *first* memcg, `sc->nr.file_taken` gets incremented.  Then, in the *second* memcg, if we scan entirely dirty unqueued folios, `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` gets incremented. The crucial point is that  `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` will *not* be equal to `sc->nr.file_taken`, and the throttle won't be triggered, even if the second memcg is full of dirty, unqueued folios. This behavior is different from the classic LRU where writeback is throttled if the *current batch* is all unqueued dirty. With MGLRU, we only throttle if *100% of the pages taken so far* on the node at the given priority are unqueued dirty.

However, I need to backtrack. While the counters are global, the check *does* happen at the end of each `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` loop. So, *within* an lruvec scan, if we encounter 100% dirty folios, we *will* throttle. If `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` is true, we throttle. This *might* be what the author meant by "too many file cache in the coldest generation can't be evicted due to being dirty". The author chose a global counter approach.

Now, a quick sanity check of the new `writeback_throttling_sane(sc)` addition. If it's *not* sane, we still throttle with `reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK)`. Let's check this function. It sleeps for `HZ/10`, like in the classic LRU.  It increments `pgdat->nr_writeback_throttled` which prevents infinite loops. So that seems fine.

The commit message mentions that MGLRU no longer attempts to write back filesystem folios via reclaim because the flusher may not be fast enough on large systems. The MGLRU may encounter folios already under writeback and the system might OOM. 

Okay, a final check.  Does this only apply to file folios? Yes, it does. `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` only checks `sc->nr.file_taken`, so it only triggers for file folios.  However, this doesn't tell the whole picture. `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` might be greater than 0 even if `sc->nr.file_taken` is 0. But, `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` is only incremented for file folios. This makes sense.

So, in summary, the logic seems correct, but it relies on global counters and only affects file folios. This behavior represents a design choice.

    "thought": "**Analyzing MGLRU's Throttling Logic**

Okay, so I'm digging into how MGLRU interacts with the file flusher throttling, specifically looking at how dirty folios are handled. First, it's clear that `shrink_folio_list()` updates `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty` for both file and anonymous dirty folios, which is the expected behavior. Now, looking at `evict_folios()`, I see that this `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty` gets added to `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty`. But here's the kicker: `sc->nr.file_taken` is ONLY incremented for FILE folios in `scan_folios()`.

So, the critical question becomes: what happens if `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` is greater than `sc->nr.file_taken`? If the condition `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` is false, it means we *won't* trigger throttling. If we're reclaiming memory and encounter unqueued dirty anonymous memory, which means swap writeback is required, but we haven't started writeback to swap, we shouldn't be triggering the *file* flusher throttling! And this seems to be the case!

Let's break down a specific scenario: if we process *only* anonymous folios, and 100% of them are unqueued dirty, `sc->nr.file_taken` will be zero. In this case, `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` will be greater than zero, so the condition will evaluate to false, and no throttling will be triggered. But, the comment "If too many file cache in the coldest generation can't be evicted due to being dirty, wake up the flusher" is still valid. So, MGLRU's existing code does NOT specifically target the case where ALL processed *file* folios were unqueued dirty.

I'm starting to see a potential problem here. If we process 100 file folios, all unqueued dirty, then `sc->nr.file_taken` will be 100. Let's say we *also* process 50 anon folios, and 10 of them are unqueued dirty (e.g., swap), `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` becomes 110. Now, `110 == 100` is FALSE! The presence of dirty *anonymous* folios PREVENTS the throttling of *file* folios! This is the intended behavior.

Digging deeper, MGLRU's `isolate_folios()` function processes folios of ONE type (either FILE or ANON) in a single `evict_folios` call. However, the `sc` (scan control) structure is shared across *multiple* `evict_folios` calls within a single `try_to_shrink_lruvec` run. This means `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` *accumulates* across both file and anonymous folio processing. So, if anonymous folios have unqueued dirty folios, `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` will exceed `sc->nr.file_taken` even if all the file folios were unqueued. That prevents throttling.

So, the throttling, and flusher wake up conditions, are pre-existing. This patch simply *adds* the throttling to match classic LRU, but the dirty detection logic is MGLRU's.

Finally, I was concerned about memcg accumulation. However, it seems MGLRU does *not* use `shrink_node_memcgs()`. Instead, `lru_gen_shrink_node()` is used for root reclaim with MGLRU. Thus, I need to investigate the implementation of `lru_gen_shrink_node()` to see if `sc` is reset correctly.

Inside `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`, after processing the `lruvec`, I see this conditional check:

```c

...
```

Now, the crucial point is that because `sc->nr` isn't reset *per* `lruvec`, it accumulates across all the `lruvec`s processed within `shrink_many()`. This check is only performed at the end of *each* `lruvec` processing! Let me walk through an example. If I process memcg1 and encounter 100 clean file folios, `sc->nr.file_taken` becomes 100. Then, I process memcg2 and find 50 dirty file folios. `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` becomes 50, and `sc->nr.file_taken` becomes 150. Consequently, the condition in the `if` statement is *false*.

Okay, so this patch is addressing a writeback throttling discrepancy between MGLRU and the classic LRU implementation, specifically for cgroup v1 environments. Let's break this down. First, the core logic: `cgroup_reclaim()` in the `scan_control` struct determines if we are in a cgroup context. If `!writeback_throttling_sane(sc)` is true, it calls `reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK)`. This appears to be identical to what `shrink_inactive_list()` (classic LRU's equivalent) does. The question becomes, does MGLRU's `shrink_folio_list()` behave the same?

Looking at the MGLRU implementation, `shrink_folio_list()` is indeed used internally, specifically in a loop. Thus, MGLRU *does* utilize `shrink_folio_list()`, which also invokes the "wait on writeback" logic. The commit message indicates that this mechanism is important, but there's a possibility of off-by-one errors or missing side-effects.

The code in the patch specifically checks: `if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken)`. If true, it wakes up the flusher threads using `wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN)` and *then* throttles reclaim via `reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK)` if `!writeback_throttling_sane(sc)` holds. The `lruvec_pgdat(lruvec)` function correctly returns the `pgdat` for the `lruvec`, which is then used by `reclaim_throttle`. This implies node-level throttling because `pgdat` contains wait queues and throttling stats. It directly mirrors the classic LRU behavior. There are no other code changes.

Now, let's verify `writeback_throttling_sane(sc)`. This function is key. It returns `true` if *not* in cgroup reclaim, or if we are using cgroup v2. Therefore, for cgroup v1, it correctly returns `false`, leading to `!writeback_throttling_sane(sc)` being `true`. This matches the commit message perfectly. Thus, cgroup v1 *will* cause throttling. The mathematics and logic are sound.

The lruvec passed to `try_to_shrink_lruvec(struct lruvec *lruvec, struct scan_control *sc)` is a parameter of the function; the `wakeup_flusher_threads` and `reclaim_throttle` functions are sleepable and safe. There are no undocumented side effects. It's a self-contained fix adding a missing throttle condition to MGLRU.

In terms of RCU, preemption, and locking, the code uses `cond_resched()` within a loop, meaning it can sleep safely. `wakeup_flusher_threads` and `reclaim_throttle` are sleepable. Thus, the context is safe. `sc->nr` doesn't need locks, as it's a local variable. The values can't overflow.

The `folio_wait_writeback(folio)` function *does* wait, within `shrink_folio_list()`, if `!writeback_throttling_sane(sc)`. Thus the patch does exactly what is specified. There is no missing component.

A slight edge case involves cgroup v1 and node-wide throttling (`pgdat->reclaim_wait[VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK]`). A cgroup v1 process might sleep on the node's waitqueue if it hits the limit, but this is consistent with how the classic LRU functions, and so is valid. This isn't a *problem*, it's consistent.

Essentially, there are no identified technical concerns with this patch. It exactly mirrors existing logic in a safe context and behaves as intended.

Okay, let's break this down. My initial understanding is that `sort_folio` identifies dirty file folios and increments `sc->nr.file_taken` and `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty`. However, if the folio is dirty and it moves it to a higher generation or another list (`folio_inc_gen`), it returns `true`, which means it *doesn't* get isolated, essentially skipping it for immediate reclaim. If `writeback` is needed, this happens. If it returns `false`, `isolate_folio` is called.

Now, `evict_folios()` is the key. It isolates folios and then passes the list to `shrink_folio_list`. This also adds to `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty`. This is where the core logic resides, and where my understanding of the interactions starts to coalesce.

The critical observation comes from the patch in `try_to_shrink_lruvec`. It checks if `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` equals `sc->nr.file_taken`. If so, and if `writeback_throttling_sane(sc)` is false, it calls `reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK)`. This seems to suggest we're throttling based on the `pgdat` of a NUMA node.

`try_to_shrink_lruvec` runs in a loop, calling `evict_folios()`. The concern arises when `cgroup_reclaim(sc)` is true, and the check for `writeback_throttling_sane(sc)` fails. The commit message states it's intended to avoid OOM by throttling reclaim, but the throttling happens on a NUMA node.

The question then becomes: is this the correct behavior for memcg reclaim, where the reclaim is happening inside the cgroup? The commit message seems targeted for cgroup v1 to avoid OOM, but the throttling logic is global and not specific to the cgroup.

To confirm my understanding, I'm checking similar logic within the classic LRU via `shrink_inactive_list`. It calls `reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK)` under the same condition, using `pgdat` from `lruvec_pgdat(lruvec)`. This confirms that the throttling is node-wide, regardless of which LRU we're shrinking.

Now, the potential issue: could `try_to_shrink_lruvec` call `reclaim_throttle` while holding a lock? The callers are `shrink_one` and `lru_gen_shrink_lruvec`.     "thought": "**Analyzing the Page Reclaim Throttle**

Okay, so I'm digging into this page reclaim logic and I think I've spotted something interesting, a potential flaw even. It looks like `sc->nr.file_taken` and `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` get updated within `sort_folio`, specifically when dealing with dirty file pages. That makes sense, right? We're isolating those folios.

`sort_folio` returns `true` for file dirty pages, and it moves them to a new generation. They *don't* end up being sent to `shrink_folio_list`. That means that `shrink_folio_list` won't increment `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty` for them. This means that `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken` are updated directly in `sort_folio` itself, good to know.

Later on, after the `evict_folios` loop, `try_to_shrink_lruvec` does a check. If `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` equals `sc->nr.file_taken`, it wakes up flusher threads and, if some conditions are met, throttles writeback using `reclaim_throttle`.

Here's where it gets interesting, and potentially problematic. It hits me that these counts, `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken`, accumulate across *all* `lruvec`s and nodes scanned during the entire lifetime of this `scan_control` (`sc`).

So, if `sc` scans `lruvec_A` and finds 10 dirty file pages, it increments both counters by 10. Then it moves to `lruvec_B`. If, for example, the scan loop is short-circuited (say, `nr_to_scan <= 0`), the check fires. It would see `10 == 10` and then tries to throttle the current node. This means it throttles `pgdat` (which could be Node B).

Here's the rub - Does it throttle Node A, where the dirty pages actually *were*, or Node B, the current node being scanned? It throttles the `pgdat` of `lruvec_B`! And that is exactly what I saw happen because `cgroup_reclaim(sc)` is true, only one cgroup gets scanned per loop. This means that a memcg that doesn't *have* any pages can get throttled.

So if `try_to_shrink_lruvec` is called for memcg_A, and it hits that `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` check, it *throttles*. That's fine for memcg_A. Okay, so I've been digging into the memory management code, specifically focusing on the interactions between MGLRU and the reclaim process. Immediately, I saw something concerning. `shrink_many()` doesn't seem to clear `sc->nr`, and neither does `shrink_one`! This becomes an immediate red flag.

Diving deeper, I noticed that `lru_gen_shrink_lruvec` is called when it *isn't* a root reclaim, i.e., it's a memcg reclaim. And it doesn't clear `sc->nr` either. This means `sc->nr` values, particularly `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken`, are accumulating across multiple `lruvec`s and memcgs. This becomes important.

In the classic LRU, I recall, the `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty == nr_taken` comparison happened locally, after the loop but before adding to `sc->nr`. However, in MGLRU, `try_to_shrink_lruvec` uses these *global* `sc->nr` variables directly in the check: `if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken)`.

Now, imagine this scenario: `memcg_A` has 10 dirty pages, and the scan adds 10 to `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken`. Then, we move to `memcg_B`. `try_to_shrink_lruvec(lruvec_B, sc)` is called. If `memcg_B` is empty, the inner loop quickly breaks because `nr_to_scan <= 0`. But, here's the kicker: the check `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` is still true because it has the *accumulated* values from `memcg_A` (10 == 10). It ends up throttling the *entire* pgdat for `memcg_B`, even though `memcg_B` has no dirty pages.

This seems like a critical bug: If you have a deep hierarchy with multiple memcgs and only the first has dirty pages, the code is going to call `reclaim_throttle` repeatedly for every empty memcg in the hierarchy, potentially sleeping repeatedly! `reclaim_throttle` blocks until a timeout or a wakeup. If this timeout is short (e.g., `HZ/10`), it could sleep 999 times or more, causing significant latency or a potential stall.

So, I re-examined `try_to_shrink_lruvec` in MGLRU. Is there a way to track `file_taken` *locally*? No, the patch only uses the *global* `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty`. But wait, the original code already included the check `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken`, and this was already using the accumulated `sc` counters. This would have already exhibited the flaw to some extent!

However, `wakeup_flusher_threads` is lightweight, but `reclaim_throttle` *blocks*. If `reclaim_throttle` blocks 999 times, it is catastrophic.

So, the patch didn't introduce the bug, it simply made it worse by adding `reclaim_throttle`.

Looking at `try_to_shrink_lruvec` again, it's clear `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` is not cleared. It accumulates over the entire `scan_control` lifetime (except for root reclaim). It also appears the critical variables are updated in `isolate_folio` and `shrink_folio_list`. This also explains what's happening.

Okay, so I've been tracing the logic around dirty page throttling in MGLRU and I'm seeing some significant issues. Initially, I see `shrink_many` calls `shrink_one`, which then calls `try_to_shrink_lruvec`. The critical point is that `sc->nr` (specifically `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken`) is *not* cleared within the loop. This means the values accumulate across cgroups. `try_to_shrink_lruvec` does a check on `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken`.

The core logic of the original code, as far as I can see, was: If the ratio of dirty, unqueued pages equals file pages, then wakeup flushers. The patch adds a blocking `reclaim_throttle` call in this very same check. The problem is, if we have memcg_A with some dirty file pages, and memcg_B with no dirty file pages, the counters accumulate and the throttle logic goes haywire.

Let's say memcg_A has 10 unqueued, dirty pages, and the condition is now `10 == 10`. That's fine, flushers are woken, and it can throttle. Then, it moves to memcg_B, which has no dirty file pages.  The counters are unchanged, so the condition remains `10 == 10`. So the bug causes memcg_B to artificially throttle, even though it doesn't have a problem at all.

This is where the issue gets exponentially worse, especially in cgroup v1 reclaim. `shrink_node_memcgs` can iterate over many descendants if there's a deep hierarchy. The accumulation of the counters can lead to multiple repeated sleeps and throttles on memory cgroups, as the condition keeps staying true until it is reset.

Looking at the code, in classic LRU (`shrink_inactive_list`), this is avoided because `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty` which is used in `shrink_inactive_list` is a LOCAL variable. In MGLRU, `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` is translated into `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty`. So it's not local to a single lruvec. In classic LRU, `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty == nr_taken` where `nr_taken` is the *total* number of pages taken, and `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty` is *locally* tracked. But in classic LRU it would be `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty == nr_taken`, where `nr_taken` is *all* file pages isolated! Thus it has a *totally different* meaning and is far more aggressive.

Okay, here's what I've got after digging into this MGLRU code. The current logic to determine when to throttle writeback in `try_to_shrink_lruvec` appears to have a critical flaw, which introduces the possibility of excessive throttling.

Essentially, I see a few key things happening here:

*   We're checking if the number of unqueued dirty pages equals the total number of file pages considered for eviction. `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` When this condition is met, we trigger a throttle. The idea, I think, is that if we've taken many file pages for eviction and they are unqueued dirty, the flusher may be too slow and throttling is necessary. This is analogous to how things worked in the old LRU code.
*   However, `sc->nr.file_taken` accumulates across multiple memcg scans within a single node reclaim. The main issue is that `sc->nr.file_taken` and `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` from a previous memcg can still persist into the next. If we hit the throttling condition for one memcg, it *will* erroneously throttle the reclaimer on empty memcgs, or ones that have already completed an eviction scan in the node. Because the variables are *not* reset until the start of a new node scan. This is in direct contrast with the classic LRU behavior, where similar variables are local to a reclaim invocation.
*   The check is problematic because it assumes that `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` is the *only* reason we trigger a throttle when the unqueued dirty equals file taken.
*   Moreover, the current logic isn't as robust as it needs to be to determine the ideal point to throttle the flusher. The code *should* be checking other conditions when it is encountering the `sc->nr.file_taken` and `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` states. It is too simplistic and does not take into account the multiple stages of a page that is being written back (queued, writeback, not yet queued dirty, clean, etc...).
*   The original patch message says something about pages that are under writeback. If the pages are truly *under writeback*, meaning the writeback has started, then `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` is *not* incremented for them. The critical check uses `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty`. Consequently, pages that are already under writeback won't cause the throttling logic to trigger. That is a flaw.
*   The fact that MGLRU has no logic to consider `sc->nr.immediate` in the way classic LRU does, which is used to throttle if pages are under writeback, will lead to throttling only if many pages are waiting to be written back. This might be a missing aspect of the classic LRU code, as well.
*   This also means that the throttle mechanism, because it's based on cumulative counters and a very simple comparison, could over-throttle in many scenarios. For example, if many pages are already under writeback, it won't throttle. This doesn't make sense, since it means we are leaving a state of the machine for too long.

So, in short, I'm seeing a critical race condition. This leads to the writeback throttling code being called too often. It seems the code is trying to fix the scenario of having too many pages that can't be reclaimed, but it's throttling in the wrong circumstance. It could stall the machine because it is not determining the correct state of the page (dirty, unqueued dirty, writeback...). And, it can be triggered by subsequent empty memcgs, or ones that have already been considered during eviction. We need to be careful with cumulative counters!

Specifically, I'm concerned about how the accumulated `scan_control` counters are being used, particularly `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken`.

Initially, `shrink_lruvec` calls `lru_gen_shrink_lruvec`, which calls `try_to_shrink_lruvec`. The important thing here is that `shrink_node_memcgs` iterates over *each* memcg in a hierarchy. If MGLRU is active, it calls `shrink_lruvec` for each memcg. And *this* is where things get problematic.

The code checks the condition `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` in `try_to_shrink_lruvec` to determine if throttling is needed, which in turn leads to a sleep via `reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK)`. 	if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken) {

    "thought": "**Analyzing Reclaim Throttling in the Linux Kernel**

Okay, so I'm digging into how the kernel handles reclaim throttling, and I've noticed something that warrants closer inspection. Specifically, I'm trying to understand the conditions under which we call `reclaim_throttle()`. My initial thought was that `current_may_throttle()` was the key, seeing it used within `shrink_node` like so:

```c
	if (!current_is_kswapd() && current_may_throttle() &&
	    !sc->hibernation_mode &&
	    (test_bit(LRUVEC_CGROUP_CONGESTED, &target_lruvec->flags) ||
	     test_bit(LRUVEC_NODE_CONGESTED, &target_lruvec->flags)))
		reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_CONGESTED);
```

However, looking at `shrink_inactive_list`, there's a different call to `reclaim_throttle()`:

```c
	if (stat.nr_unqueued_dirty == nr_taken) {
		wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN);
		/*
		 * For cgroupv1 dirty throttling is achieved by waking up
		 * the kernel flusher here and later waiting on folios
		 * which are in writeback to finish (see shrink_folio_list()).
		 *
		 * Flusher may not be able to issue writeback quickly
		 * enough for cgroupv1 writeback throttling to work
		 * on a large system.
		 */
		if (!writeback_throttling_sane(sc))
			reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);
	}
```

Here, `current_may_throttle()` is conspicuously *absent* before the call to `reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK)`. I'm starting to think the MGLRU logic uses an identical method. This discrepancy needs careful consideration.

My next line of thinking is whether any critical locking is being missed. I'm focusing on the call to `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`, which I find like this:

```c
	if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken) {
		struct pglist_data *pgdat = lruvec_pgdat(lruvec);

		wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN);

		if (!writeback_throttling_sane(sc))
			reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);
	}
```

The `lruvec_pgdat(lruvec)` evaluation looks solid. But, is MGLRU holding any spinlocks when it calls `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`? That's what I need to ascertain now. I should look into `lru_gen_shrink_lruvec()` to clarify that.

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Analysis of MGLRU's Throttle Logic**

Okay, let's break this down. From what I see, `lru_gen_shrink_lruvec` and `shrink_one` call `try_to_shrink_lruvec`, but they don't hold any spinlocks around `try_to_shrink_lruvec` and `evict_folios()`. This means calling `reclaim_throttle()` which sleeps, is perfectly fine.

The core of the patch revolves around this:

```c
	if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken) {
		struct pglist_data *pgdat = lruvec_pgdat(lruvec);

		wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN);

		if (!writeback_throttling_sane(sc))
			reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);
	}
```

Now, the crucial aspect is the condition `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken`. Unlike the standard LRU where `file_taken` counts all *file* pages, here `sc->nr.file_taken` is only incremented inside `evict_folios()` if the folio is both a file page *and* dirty. In effect, `sc->nr.file_taken` now counts the *dirty file folios* taken. `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` is just incremented when the file is not under writeback.

So, when the code checks `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken`, it's actually checking if *all* dirty, taken file folios are unqueued, meaning they are not under writeback. If that's the case, we should throttle reclaim, as in cgroup v1 to avoid OOM killer. The check is performed at the end of the `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` loop, meaning it checks the cumulative dirty file pages taken during this call and not one page at a time. This behavior matches `shrink_inactive_list`.

`pgdat` is initialized via `lruvec_pgdat(lruvec)` which dereferences `lruvec`, which is safe. Both `sc` and `lruvec` are used unconditionally before. `reclaim_throttle()` is safe since `reclaim_throttle` can sleep and no locks are held.

The check `writeback_throttling_sane(sc)` doesn't involve any new pointer dereferences.

There are no memory leaks, unbalanced reference counts, nor list manipulation issues.

Therefore, this patch seems solid, and no issues are detected.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Interaction of `shrink_inactive_list()` and LRU Shrinking**

Okay, let's break this down. I'm looking at how the reclaim process handles dirty pages, particularly concerning the interaction between `shrink_inactive_list()` and `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`, especially in relation to the new MGLRU code.

Initially, I noted that `shrink_inactive_list()` calls `reclaim_throttle()` *after* waking up flushers if the local `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty` equals `nr_taken`. This seems like a reasonable mechanism to throttle writeback when unqueued dirty pages match the number of pages taken for shrinking within a single call.

The first point of potential concern: I realized that `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` doesn't seem to reset the `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken` counters. This is where MGLRU's `evict_folios()` comes into play, as it calls `shrink_folio_list()`, which is important for understanding the page accounting.

So, `shrink_inactive_list()` accumulates `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken` across *each* call, within the *whole* scan. Then I wanted to know where the counters *are* reset. This brings me to the actual code. `shrink_inactive_list()` uses a local `stat` variable, and at the end of its execution, it updates the `scan_control` structure (`sc`) with these accumulated values.

Then, the critical point jumps out.  `shrink_inactive_list()` checks if `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty` is equal to `nr_taken` using *local* variables, and then triggers `wakeup_flusher_threads()` and possibly `reclaim_throttle()`. This seems fine on its own.

Now I look at the code inside `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`: Here's the key: it checks if `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` equals `sc->nr.file_taken`. The `sc->nr` is a persistent counter inside `scan_control` that accumulates values across the *entire* reclaim cycle, not just a single call. This is the difference!

The consequence of this seems to be that `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` *might* trigger writeback throttling based on an accumulation of dirty and file pages over the *entire* reclaim run, not just a single pass. This is where I start to question whether this accumulation is working as intended. Is this checking intended to operate after a single `evict_folios()` call, or potentially after multiple iterations of `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`?

And the original MGLRU code used only `wakeup_flusher_threads()` when the counters were equal. So this patch simply added `reclaim_throttle()` to this logic.

So, to summarize my concerns: `shrink_inactive_list()` uses local variables for its immediate triggering, while `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` uses accumulated values. If `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` loops, the comparison of `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken` might not be accurate, or reflect the immediate state of the page at any given iteration of the loop, because file folios may become clean, or be unevictable. The goal now is to track where `sc->nr` is cleared or initialized to fully understand this accumulation and its implications.

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**MGLRU Reclaim Throttling Bug: A Deep Dive**

Okay, so I've been tracing the logic in MGLRU's reclaim path, and I've stumbled upon a significant performance bottleneck, which is essentially a livelock situation. Here’s what I've discovered:

The functions `sort_folio()` and `shrink_folio_list()` update `sc->nr.file_taken` and `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` respectively. `file_taken` accumulates folios (pages) that are either skipped due to being dirty or are isolated, while `unqueued_dirty` keeps track of dirty folios that are not yet queued for writeback. In `scan_folios()`, `sort_folio()` handles dirty, non-evictable folios by leaving them in the LRU. Isolated folios go to `evict_folios()`, and then into `shrink_folio_list()`. `shrink_folio_list()` then also updates the `stat` variable, which then contributes to `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty`. 

Now, the core issue lies within `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`, which is called within the `shrink_many()` loop (and by `shrink_one()` and others).  Here, a check is made: `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken`. The code calls `reclaim_throttle()` which then sleeps for `HZ/10`. This check is performed *per-lruvec*, but, critically, `sc->nr.file_taken` and `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` are *global* accumulators, initialized at the start of `shrink_node()` and zeroed out only once per `shrink_node` pass.

The bug is this: if one memcg (memory control group) in the system contributes a certain amount of dirty, unqueued file pages, let's say 100, then `sc->nr.file_taken` and `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` will be equal. If *that* condition triggers the throttle (sleep), and subsequent memcgs have *no* file pages (or very few), `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` *still* checks these global counters, which are *still* equal and non-zero.  Because it's within a loop, every subsequent memcg can then trigger `reclaim_throttle()`, causing a massive, artificial stall that blocks the reclaimer thread for seconds.  I confirmed that the loop can break early (`nr_to_scan <= 0`) for various reasons.

The original code ALREADY does `wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN)` based on this condition!

Classic LRU uses local variables so it throttles ONCE per batch of pages. This is a critical difference. `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` in MGLRU operates on these shared counters.

In effect, a single memcg "poisoning" the global state can cause the reclaimer to sleep 100ms for every subsequent memcg, regardless of the individual state of those memcgs. This is a severe design flaw, and it translates into a substantial system-wide stall. It's essentially a concurrency-related livelock, where the thread is repeatedly blocked on a stale condition. It is also an incorrect use of shared state.

I've verified that lock and RCU use are correct, and no atomic context issue.

The bug is therefore: The shared state of `sc->nr` is incorrectly used in a per-lruvec function, resulting in a global stall based on an individual memcg's dirty pages.

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Analysis of a Potential Livelock in Memory Management**

Okay, let's break this down. The code in question seems to introduce a potential livelock in the reclaim path, particularly when dealing with cgroupv1 and direct reclaim. The issue stems from the interaction between `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` and the introduction of `reclaim_throttle()`.

Initially, I'm tracing the execution flow. We're checking `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` against `sc->nr.file_taken`. The problematic aspect is the accumulation of `sc->nr` variables across memcg iterations. If these values become equal in *any* memcg during a scan, `wakeup_flusher_threads()` gets called (which, fortunately, is fast and non-blocking). However, then, `reclaim_throttle()` is invoked, and that's the kicker because `reclaim_throttle()` blocks!

This blocking call, specifically `schedule_timeout(HZ/10)`, is now inside a loop that iterates through *every* memcg in a node (or a cgroup v1 hierarchy, which it also iterates across). This turns a normally non-blocking operation into a prolonged sleep, potentially for *minutes* if N, the number of memcgs, is large. This isn't a simple performance issue; it's an OOM risk and can freeze the system.

I’m meticulously examining the lock scope to make sure there are no obvious lock ordering issues related to this change. Luckily, it seems this throttle is *not* taken under a lock. It appears to be an extreme performance bug and concurrency issue.

The critical piece of the puzzle is the `writeback_throttling_sane(sc)` function.  For global direct reclaim (`!cgroup_reclaim`), this function returns true, so `reclaim_throttle()` is bypassed. **However**, for cgroupv1 reclaim (`!cgroup_subsys_on_dfl` and `cgroup_reclaim`), it returns false, *and*  `reclaim_throttle()` is called!

The real trap is that the values used in the conditional, `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken`, are *global* to the *entire* `shrink_node()` pass (they are not reset per-memcg). So, if *any* memcg hits the `unqueued_dirty == file_taken` condition, the throttle will be engaged for *every subsequent* memcg processed in that scan. The cgroup v1 reclaim will scan descendant memcgs, and if the condition holds for one, then all future shrinks also throttle, potentially indefinitely.

Moreover, if there are many memcgs in the hierarchy, this introduces a huge, accumulated delay, as `shrink_node_memcgs()` iterates through all of them. This is an O(N) livelock situation.

I'm now focusing on *why* this logic was introduced. It appears the intent was similar to classic LRU behavior. However, the author missed a crucial detail: the `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty` used in classic LRU is *local* to the iteration batch, whereas the new `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` is *global* across the `shrink_node()` call. The flaw is that a variable representing an accumulated global sum is used to determine if a potentially blocking action should occur.

Furthermore, a normal process (not a kthread, which would be in a different context) triggering cgroup v1 reclaim *will* throttle, making this even worse.

Finally, I checked whether `sc->nr` is cleared or reset in `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`.  The answer is NO! This solidifies my conclusion that this is a race condition.

Because `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` is executed per-`lruvec` (for each memory cgroup via `shrink_many()`), if one cgroup encounters 100% unqueued dirty file pages, the global `sc->nr` fields become equal. When the reclaimer moves to subsequent memcgs, it may scan no file pages (e.g., if the memcg is empty or only has anon pages), leaving the global `sc->nr` values unchanged. The condition will thus evaluate to true repeatedly for every remaining memcg in the hierarchy, invoking `reclaim_throttle()` (which sleeps for up to `HZ/10`) on every single iteration. This translates to an unintended massive artificial stall, effectively causing a livelock during direct reclaim.     "thought": "**Concurrency Logic Bug in memcg Reclaim: Livelock in `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`**

Okay, I've got a definitive bug here. The root cause is a concurrency logic error in `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`. The code is calling `reclaim_throttle()` based on a shared, accumulated state (`sc->nr`) across an entire node scan, specifically within the context of memory cgroup (memcg) reclaim.

The problem stems from this: `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` is called for *each* `lruvec` (representing a memory cgroup). The crucial condition, `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken`, uses variables inside `struct scan_control`. Critically, these variables are accumulated *globally* across the entire node scan, across *all* memcgs. They are only zeroed at the *start* of `shrink_node()`.

Here's the problem: if a single memcg triggers this condition (e.g., 100% unqueued dirty file pages), the condition will remain true for all subsequent `lruvec`s processed during the rest of the node scan. Consequently, `reclaim_throttle()` will be erroneously invoked repeatedly for *every remaining* memcg in the hierarchy. This creates a massive artificial stall, a livelock, during direct reclaim, sleeping up to `HZ/10`. Classic LRU implementations correctly check this condition against the *local* statistics of the *current* batch. This implementation does not.

Let's address my checklist for a concurrency issue.

*   **Locking/RCU:** No `rcu_read_lock()` is held during `reclaim_throttle()`. No spinlocks or `mutex`es are involved. No issues there. This is a clear "Missed consideration of shared state concurrency leading to livelock".
*   **`writeback_throttling_sane()`:** I checked `writeback_throttling_sane()`. In cgroup v1, if we are doing cgroup *limit* reclaim, which is what is happening here, this becomes `false`, triggering the problematic code.
*   **Invalid `pgdat`:** The `pgdat` pointer itself is always valid. `reclaim_throttle()` also checks `pgdat->reclaim_wait` which is good.
*   **Sleeping in Atomic Context/Locks:** I've traced the call chain: `lru_gen_shrink_lruvec()` -> `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`. This is called from `shrink_lruvec()` and that is called from `shrink_node_memcgs()`. There are block plugs involved, but sleeping within them is supported and expected. No lock is held within the loop.
*   **`rcu_read_lock()`:** In `shrink_node_memcgs()`, `memcg = mem_cgroup_iter(...)` is used which does NOT hold an RCU lock. I traced through `shrink_many()`, and confirms that `rcu_read_unlock()` is correctly called *before* `shrink_one()` and therefore before `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`. No RCU locks are held during the critical code execution.

This is a clear thread-safety issue: a global shared counter used for a local loop iteration's decision, causing livelock. This is incorrect handling of state across multiple iterative executions.

Okay, so here's what I've got. I'm looking at this `HZ/10` timeout in MGLRU and it's hitting me hard. It seems the core of the problem lies in how the writeback throttling is implemented. It's using this `sc->nr` – the global accumulator. The condition in `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` is what's throwing the whole thing off: `if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken)`. I'm starting to see a pattern.

If we have one `memcg_A` and during a scan, there are only unqueued dirty file folios, the accumulated `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` becomes equal to `sc->nr.file_taken`. So far, so good, a sleep for HZ/10 happens. Now, the loop moves to a different `memcg_B`. But here's the kicker:  `sc->nr` doesn't get cleared between memcg scans. If this `memcg_B` is empty, or doesn't have any file pages to scan, the critical condition remains *true*. The reclaimer sleeps *again* for `HZ/10`. I can already see the problem!

It's clear that if this repeats for every subsequent empty or non-file memcg in the hierarchy, this means potentially seconds of *uninterruptible* sleep. It's a livelock! And it's all due to the global `sc->nr` accumulator.  `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` doesn't reset it, nor does `shrink_many()`. The whole thing boils down to the fact that the patch is incorrectly using the global `sc->nr` accumulators.

Let's break it down: `shrink_node()` initializes `sc->nr`.  Then, `lru_gen_shrink_node()` calls `shrink_many()`, which iterates through memcgs calling `shrink_one()` -> `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`. Inside `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`, we isolate folios, accumulate to `sc->nr`, and then *at the end*, we check the faulty condition, which means we throttle for `HZ/10`. Since there's no reset, it accumulates totals across ALL memcgs in the node.  This means that after memcg_A has set `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` == `sc->nr.file_taken`, it will stay true, and we sleep in EVERY remaining memcg.

I've traced back the logic and can confirm: the global `sc->nr` is definitely *not* cleared per memcg within `shrink_many()` or `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`.  Classic LRU uses *local* batch statistics via `struct reclaim_stat stat`. This means the throttling check is on the *local* batch, not the global accumulator, which is the source of the bug. It's a semantic mismatch and it introduces severe over-throttling. The condition is incorrectly comparing global accumulators instead of the local batch totals. This results in a *severe* performance issue. This could be High/Critical because it's a regression that can stall the whole system under the right conditions!

The crux of the issue revolves around how `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken` are updated within the MGLRU context. `sc->nr.file_taken` gets incremented when a dirty file page is scanned, and if not under writeback, `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` is incremented. However, `sc->nr.file_taken` also increases for *clean* file pages isolated by MGLRU.

So, here's the problem. `sc->nr.file_taken` now includes *all* isolated file pages, clean, dirty and writeback. Therefore, `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` holds true only if *every* isolated page in the pass is an `unqueued_dirty` page. This means that if even a single clean or writeback file page is seen *anywhere* in the node scan, `file_taken` will be greater than `unqueued_dirty`, and this condition becomes permanently false for the rest of the scan.

*   If the condition is ever false, the throttle is disabled for the entire `shrink_node` pass, even if subsequent memcgs are full of dirty pages. This means no throttling.

Since `file_taken` and `unqueued_dirty` don't change, the condition stays true, and the reclaimer sleeps repeatedly (livestock).

This breaks the intention:

Author: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>

mm: vmscan: fix dirty folios throttling on cgroup v1 for MGLRU

This commit enables dirty folio throttling on cgroup v1 for MGLRU by
checking if evicted file folios are predominantly dirty and unqueued,
waking the flusher, and throttling reclaim.

```
diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c
--- a/mm/vmscan.c
+++ b/mm/vmscan.c
@@ -5023,9 +5023,24 @@ static bool try_to_shrink_lruvec(struct lruvec *lruvec, struct scan_control *sc)

+		if (!writeback_throttling_sane(sc))

The patch adds a check `if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken)` inside MGLRU's `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` to throttle direct reclaim for cgroup v1.
2. `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` is invoked repeatedly for each memory cgroup during `shrink_many()`. However, the `sc->nr` fields are global accumulators that collect statistics across the entire `shrink_node()` pass; they are not zeroed between memcg iterations.
3. If an early memcg yields only unqueued dirty file folios, `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken` become equal. For all subsequent memcgs that yield zero file folios (e.g., if they are empty or only contain anonymous pages), these global accumulators remain unchanged. The condition continues to evaluate to true, causing the reclaimer to mistakenly sleep for `HZ/10` (100ms) for *every* such memcg. In a deep hierarchy, this creates massive multi-second latency spikes (an O(N) stall).
